perturbation.original_text: 16. The Disclosing Party retains sole ownership and title to the Information and Materials at all times. The Recipient shall k eep the Materials free of liens, attachments, and other encumbrances.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. The Disclosing Party generally retains ownership and title to the Information and Materials, unless otherwise agreed upon in a separate written agreement. The Recipient should make an effort to keep the Materials reasonably free of liens, attachments, and other significant encumbrances.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless otherwise agreed upon in a separate written agreement' allows for the possibility of the Recipient gaining ownership, contradicting 'sole ownership and title'. Also, 'shall keep' is changed to 'should make an effort to keep reasonably free', weakening the original obligation. This creates an in-text contradiction in this section itself. The first part mentions sole ownership which later on says about written agreement to change the ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Disclosing Party generally retains ownership and title to the Information and Materials, unless otherwise agreed upon in a separate written agreement. The Recipient should make an effort to keep the Materials reasonably free of liens, attachments, and other significant encumbrances.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text states 'The Disclosing Party retains sole ownership and title to the Information and Materials at all times', implying that the Recipient never gains ownership. The changed text adds 'unless otherwise agreed upon in a separate written agreement', allowing for the possibility of the Recipient gaining ownership. This directly contradicts the original assertion of 'sole ownership and title'. Furthermore, the original text requires the Recipient to 'shall keep the Materials free of liens, attachments, and other encumbrances', while the changed text weakens this obligation to 'should make an effort to keep the Materials reasonably free of liens, attachments, and other significant encumbrances', which means that there is no longer a guarantee that the Recipient will keep the materials free of these things, creating an in-text contradiction. Because the Changed text is the same as the Contradicted text, the contradiction is within the section itself.

perturbation.original_text: 19. In default of any of the above obligations and stipulations, the Buyers shall immediately forfeit a fine payable to the Vendor, without a warning or notice of default being required, to t he amount of 250,000 Euro without prejudice to the Buyers' obligation to compensate the Vendor fully for damages sustained. Note this penalty clause may not be enforceable in all jurisdictions .
perturbation.changed_text: 19. In default of any of the above obligations and stipulations, the Buyers may be subject to a fine payable to the Vendor, provided that a warning or notice of default has been issued and a reasonable period for remediation has been given, up to a maximum amount of 250,000 Euro, which shall serve as liquidated damages, precluding any further claims for actual damages sustained. Note this clause may not be enforceable in all jurisdictions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall immediately forfeit' to 'may be subject to' and adding a requirement for 'warning or notice of default' and 'reasonable period for remediation' fundamentally alters the penalty for default. It now requires warning where before it did not and may not be enforced. Also, the 'fine' which before allowed the Vendor to still request to be compensated fully is now consider 'liquidated damages'. This contradicts the original clause where no warning was required and the vendor can request for compensation above 250,000 euros. This happens in the section itself where both are contradict eachother.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In default of any of the above obligations and stipulations, the Buyers may be subject to a fine payable to the Vendor, provided that a warning or notice of default has been issued and a reasonable period for remediation has been given, up to a maximum amount of 250,000 Euro, which shall serve as liquidated damages, precluding any further claims for actual damages sustained. Note this clause may not be enforceable in all jurisdictions.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text states that in default of obligations, the buyer 'shall immediately forfeit a fine'. The changed text alters this to 'may be subject to a fine' and adds requirements for 'warning or notice of default' and a 'reasonable period for remediation'. The original text did not require warning or notice of default and allowed the vendor to request for compensation above 250,000 euros, while the changed text considers the fine as 'liquidated damages', precluding further claims for actual damages sustained. Therefore, the modified text creates a direct conflict with the clause where no warning was required and the vendor can request compensation above 250,000 euros, making it an in-text contradiction according to the 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' definition.
